narrative:
On June 30, 2026, the Karnataka High Court recorded an overall hearing rate of 41.2%, with 974 cases heard out of 2,362 scheduled. This reflects a slight decline in activity compared to the 44.4% hearing rate observed on June 29. The court maintained a bias gap of -5.7%, as commoner-related matters saw a higher hearing conversion (42.4%) than government-related matters (36.7%).

Judge-wise performance showed significant variance. Hall 8 (H.P. Sandesh) recorded a high efficiency rate of 79.1%, while Hall 6 (B.M. Shyam Prasad) reported zero hearings across all 153 scheduled cases. Other benches, such as those in Hall 20 (Shivashankar Amarnnavar) and Hall 21 (V. Srishananda), maintained strong productivity with hearing rates of 64.8% and 68.2%, respectively. 

Hearing stage conversion remains centered on procedural progression. Orders (52.5%) and Admissions (53.0%) saw the highest conversion rates, while Final Hearings lagged at 28.3%. Preliminary Hearings saw a conversion rate of 23.0%, continuing the trend where interlocutory and admission-stage matters take precedence over final disposals.

Case type distribution was dominated by Writ Petitions (WP), which accounted for 1,017 scheduled cases but only a 32.4% hearing rate, showing a marked decline from the 41.6% rate observed on June 29. Clearance was most efficient in niche categories such as regular petitions (100%) and criminal revision petitions (80.0%), while miscellaneous first appeals (MFA) sustained a steady 51.6% clearance rate. Despite the volume of writ petitions, the court’s focus on orders and admissions continues to define its current operational capacity.
